title: Why Integrating PA, LNA, and Switch on One GaN Die Is Harder Than It Looks
gist: Viettel Semiconductor explains why single-die GaN-on-SiC front-end modules (integrating PA, LNA, and switch for X-band T/R modules) are a hard integration problem rather than a floorplanning exercise, driven by isolation, thermal coupling, and compounded process yield risk, and positions its VPF1010 X-band FEM as silicon-proven with measured (not simulated) data across these failure modes.

themes.theme: Why single-die PA+LNA+switch integration is hard

insights.claim: Getting 40+ dB of isolation on a shared GaN die between a multi-watt PA and a noise-floor-sensitive LNA, without a mechanical relay or off-chip switch, forces trade-offs in switch topology (series-shunt vs. traveling-wave) that cost insertion loss and linearity elsewhere, since any leakage through the switch or substrate coupling desensitizes or damages the receive chain.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Identifies isolation (not raw gain) as the binding electrical constraint in single-die GaN FEM design, a specific technical bar competing GaN IP/module vendors must clear to credibly claim production-qualified single-die integration.
insights.quote: Getting 40+ dB of isolation on a shared die, without a mechanical relay or an off-chip switch to lean on, forces trade-offs in switch topology (series-shunt vs. traveling-wave) that cost insertion loss and linearity elsewhere.
insights.locator: Isolation, not gain, is the bottleneck

insights.claim: Thermal coupling on a single die is a system problem hiding as a device problem: the PA runs hot and the LNA sits in the PA's thermal gradient rather than a controlled ambient, so bias circuits that appear stable in simulation at 25°C can drift once the PA has been keyed for a few hundred milliseconds nearby — a failure mode that typically only shows up under real silicon and real duty cycles, not in simulation.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Explains why many 'integrated FEM' datasheets stop at post-layout simulation and never publish measured PA-on/LNA-noise-figure data — a specific due-diligence red flag for evaluators comparing integrated GaN FEM vendor claims.
insights.quote: This is the failure mode that doesn't show up until you have real silicon under real duty cycles – which is exactly why so many "integrated FEM" datasheets stop at post-layout simulation and never publish measured PA-on/LNA-noise-figure data.
insights.locator: Thermal coupling is a system problem hiding in a device problem

insights.claim: Putting a PA, LNA, and switch on one die means process yield is the product of three sub-yields rather than the yield of the best single block, since a defect or process corner that only marginally affects the PA can kill the LNA's noise-figure spec — the underlying reason single-die FEMs are harder to bring to volume than three separate best-of-breed die in a module.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Quantifies the compounding-yield-risk mechanism behind single-die GaN FEM manufacturability, a key cost/volume-ramp consideration for program managers weighing single-die integration against discrete multi-die modules.
insights.quote: Putting all three on one die means a defect or process corner that only marginally affects the PA can kill the LNA's noise figure spec, and now your yield is the product of three sub-yields instead of the yield of your best block.
insights.locator: Process yield compounds every block's risk

insights.claim: Viettel Semiconductor's VPF1010 X-band GaN-on-SiC front-end module integrates PA, LNA, and switch on a single die and is characterized and silicon-proven — with measured (not simulated or 'expected') TX-to-RX isolation, RX noise figure with the PA keyed nearby, and switch insertion loss versus temperature — which it argues does the hard qualification work for module-level integrators before they receive a sample.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: A vendor claim of measured (versus simulated) data across the specific failure modes it just described (isolation, thermal-coupled noise figure, yield) is a differentiated marketing claim worth verifying directly against Viettel's published VPF1010 datasheet before treating as validated.
insights.quote: That's the bar we designed VPF1010, our X-band GaN-on-SiC FEM, against: PA, LNA, and switch integrated on a single die, characterized and silicon-proven rather than simulated.
